questions.q: Why must a moduli functor $\mathcal{M}:\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$ for elliptic curves fail to be representable?
choices: because elliptic curves are not projective
because the functor lands in $\mathrm{Grpd}$, not $\mathrm{Set}$, so representability is impossible by definition
because elliptic curves have non-trivial automorphisms (at least $[-1]$), so twisting families along covers produces distinct families with the same classifying map
because the $j$-line is not a scheme
questions.hint: Every elliptic curve has the automorphism $[-1]$; twisting a family by this automorphism along a cover produces a non-isomorphic family with the same classifying map to any putative fine moduli…
questions.explain: Every elliptic curve has the automorphism $[-1]$; twisting a family by this automorphism along a cover produces a non-isomorphic family with the same classifying map to any putative fine moduli scheme. Passing to groupoid-valued functors is precisely what records this data.

questions.q: A fibered category $\mathcal{X}\to\mathrm{Sch}$ is a stack (in the étale topology) iff for every cover $\{U_i\to U\}$:
choices: the presheaf $V\mapsto \mathrm{Iso}(x|_V, y|_V)$ is a sheaf, and descent data $(x_i,\varphi_{ij})$ satisfying the cocycle glue to a unique (up to unique iso) object on $U$
every object of $\mathcal{X}(U)$ is isomorphic to an object pulled back from a single $U_i$
the category $\mathcal{X}(U)$ is discrete (has no non-identity isomorphisms)
$\mathcal{X}(U) \to \prod_i \mathcal{X}(U_i)$ is an equivalence of categories with no compatibility conditions
questions.hint: The stack axiom has two layers: isomorphism sheaves must glue locally, and objects given by compatible local data (a descent datum satisfying the cocycle on triple overlaps) must glue to a global…
questions.explain: The stack axiom has two layers: isomorphism sheaves must glue locally, and objects given by compatible local data (a descent datum satisfying the cocycle on triple overlaps) must glue to a global object unique up to unique isomorphism.

questions.q: Let $G$ be a groupoid with three objects, each having automorphism group $\mathbb{Z}/2$, all connected by isomorphisms. How many isomorphism classes does $\pi_0(G)$ have?
questions.explain: All three objects are connected by isomorphisms, so they form a single isomorphism class: $\pi_0(G)$ has one element. The three $\mathbb{Z}/2$ automorphism groups are what is lost when one collapses to $\pi_0$.

hard.q: A fibered category $p:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{C}$ satisfies descent for a cover $\{U_i\to U\}$ but the isomorphism-sheaf condition fails for some pair of objects. Which axiom of a stack does this violate?
choices: The gluing axiom for objects
The sheaf axiom for morphisms (isomorphisms must form a sheaf)
The Yoneda lemma for stacks
Representability of the diagonal
hard.hint: Descent is effective only if isomorphism sheaves are actually sheaves.
hard.explain: A stack requires two conditions: (1) $\mathrm{Isom}(x,y)$ is a sheaf (morphism sheaf axiom) and (2) descent data for objects glue. Failure of the Isom sheaf condition violates the first axiom while leaving the second intact.

hard.q: Which of these is a counterexample showing that the naive moduli set-functor $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}^{\mathrm{naive}}:\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$ (assigning iso-classes of elliptic curves) is NOT a sheaf in the étale topology?
choices: The $j$-invariant takes values in $\mathbb{A}^1$, which is a scheme, so it is automatically a sheaf
An isotrivial elliptic surface over $\mathbb{P}^1$ can have constant $j$-invariant yet be non-trivial, so local triviality does not imply global triviality
Every elliptic curve has a unique model, so descent is trivially satisfied
Étale covers do not exist for elliptic curves
hard.explain: Twisting a constant family by a non-trivial $\mu_2$-torsor (via the $[-1]$ automorphism) over an étale cover produces an isotrivial but non-trivial family. The cocycle data exist, but they glue to different isomorphism-class representatives depending on the chosen descent isomorphisms — violating sheafness.

hard.q: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be the groupoid with objects $\{A,B,C\}$, morphisms forming a single isomorphism class, and $\mathrm{Aut}(A)=\{e\}$. After passing to $\pi_0$, how many objects remain?
hard.hint: Automorphism groups give isotropy, not multiplicity.
hard.explain: All three objects are isomorphic, so they represent a single isomorphism class. $|\pi_0(\mathcal{C})|=1$. The trivial automorphism groups are irrelevant to $\pi_0$.

questions.q: The classifying stack $BG$ is defined by $BG(R)=$ groupoid of $G$-torsors on $\mathrm{Spec}\,R$. Why can $BG$ not be a scheme?
choices: because torsors are not quasi-coherent sheaves
because $G$-torsors form a set, not a groupoid
because the unique geometric point $\mathrm{Spec}\,\overline{k}\to BG$ (the trivial torsor) has automorphism group $G$, and schemes cannot have non-trivial automorphisms at a geometric point
because $BG$ is never finite type
questions.explain: A scheme's functor of points lands in sets, so its geometric points have trivial automorphism groups. $BG$ has automorphism group $G$ at its trivial torsor, which records pure automorphism data and prevents representability by any scheme.

questions.q: How many isomorphism classes of $\mathbb{Z}/3$-torsors are there on $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q$ for a finite field $\mathbb{F}_q$? (Equivalently, $|H^1(\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q,\mathbb{Z}/3)|$, using that the absolute Galois group of $\mathbb{F}_q$ is $\widehat{\mathbb{Z}}$ and $\mathrm{Hom}(\widehat{\mathbb{Z}},\mathbb{Z}/3)=\mathbb{Z}/3$.)
questions.explain: $\mathrm{Hom}_{\text{cont}}(\widehat{\mathbb{Z}},\mathbb{Z}/3)\cong \mathbb{Z}/3$ has 3 elements, one for each element of $\mathbb{Z}/3$. Hence $|\pi_0(B(\mathbb{Z}/3)(\mathbb{F}_q))|=3$.

questions.q: For $G=\mathbb{Z}/2$ over $\mathbb{R}$: $\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R})=\mathbb{Z}/2$, so $|\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)|=?$ (this counts iso classes of $\mathbb{Z}/2$-torsors over $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{R}$).
questions.explain: $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)=\{0,\mathrm{id}\}$ has 2 elements: the trivial torsor and the non-trivial one corresponding to $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{C}\to\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{R}$.

hard.q: For any algebraic group $G$, the inertia stack $I(BG)=BG\times_{BG\times BG}BG$ is equivalent to which stack?
choices: $BG$ itself
The adjoint quotient stack $[G/G]$ where $G$ acts by conjugation
The classifying stack $B(G\times G)$
The quotient scheme $G/G$
hard.hint: The inertia stack parameterizes objects with an automorphism.
hard.explain: The inertia stack of $BG$ parametrises pairs (torsor, automorphism), which is the same data as a $G$-torsor together with a section of the associated group scheme — i.e., an element of $G$ up to conjugation. This is exactly $[G/G]$ for the conjugation action.

hard.q: For $G=\mathbb{Z}/5$ over an algebraically closed field $k$ with $\mathrm{char}(k)\ne 5$, how many isomorphism classes of $G$-torsors are there over $\mathrm{Spec}\,k$?
hard.explain: Over an algebraically closed field, $H^1_{\text{ét}}(\mathrm{Spec}\,k,G)=0$ for any finite group $G$ of order coprime to the characteristic. So there is exactly $1$ isomorphism class: the trivial torsor.

hard.q: The correct chain of implications among stack conditions is:
choices: Scheme $\Rightarrow$ Algebraic space $\Rightarrow$ DM stack $\Rightarrow$ Artin stack
Artin stack $\Rightarrow$ DM stack $\Rightarrow$ Algebraic space $\Rightarrow$ Scheme
DM stack $\Rightarrow$ Scheme $\Rightarrow$ Algebraic space $\Rightarrow$ Artin stack
Algebraic space $\Rightarrow$ Scheme $\Rightarrow$ DM stack $\Rightarrow$ Artin stack
hard.hint: Separated implies quasi-separated; Artin is weaker than Deligne-Mumford.
hard.explain: Every scheme is an algebraic space (trivial groupoid structure); every algebraic space is a DM stack (étale atlas, trivial automorphisms); every DM stack is an Artin stack (étale implies smooth). The reverse implications all fail: $B\mathbb{G}_m$ is Artin but not DM; $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_2]$ is DM but not an algebraic space.

questions.q: A $T$-point of the quotient stack $[X/G]$ is:
choices: a morphism $T\to X/G$ to the quotient scheme
a pair $(P\to T,\ \phi:P\to X)$ consisting of a $G$-torsor on $T$ and a $G$-equivariant map $\phi$ to $X$
a $G$-invariant function $T\to X$
a free $G$-action on $T$
questions.explain: By definition $[X/G](T)$ is the groupoid of pairs $(P\to T,\ \phi:P\to X)$ with $P$ a $G$-torsor and $\phi$ equivariant. Setting $X=\mathrm{pt}$ recovers $BG$; setting $G=\{e\}$ recovers $X$ itself.

questions.q: When does $[X/G]$ coincide (as stacks) with the quotient scheme $X/G$?
choices: always, since stacks are just schemes in disguise
precisely when the action of $G$ on $X$ is free (trivial stabilisers at every geometric point)
only when $G$ is abelian
never, unless $G$ is trivial
questions.explain: If the action is free, every stabiliser is trivial and no automorphism data needs to be remembered, so $[X/G]$ is the ordinary quotient $X/G$. Non-free actions create non-trivial stabilisers and force stacky structure.

questions.q: For $G=\mu_3$ acting on $\mathbb{A}^1$ by $\zeta\cdot x = \zeta x$, the stabiliser at $x=0$ is all of $\mu_3$ and is trivial elsewhere. What is the order of the automorphism group of the origin in $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_3]$?
questions.explain: $\mathrm{Stab}_{\mu_3}(0)=\mu_3$ has order $3$. Geometrically this is the stacky enhancement at the fixed point; away from $0$ the quotient is an ordinary scheme.

hard.q: Let $G=\mathbb{G}_m$ act on $X=\mathbb{A}^2\setminus\{0\}$ by $\lambda\cdot(x,y)=(\lambda x,\lambda y)$. The quotient stack $[X/G]$ is equivalent to which classical geometric object?
choices: The projective line $\mathbb{P}^1$, since the action is free
The classifying stack $B\mathbb{G}_m$
The weighted projective line $\mathbb{P}(1,1)$, confirming $[X/G]\cong\mathbb{P}^1$ as a DM stack
The affine line $\mathbb{A}^1$
hard.hint: The scheme-theoretic quotient is $\mathbb{P}^1$; stacky data is trivial here.
hard.explain: $\mathbb{G}_m$ acts freely on $\mathbb{A}^2\setminus\{0\}$ (no non-trivial stabilisers), so $[X/G]$ is an algebraic space and in fact coincides with the ordinary quotient scheme $\mathbb{P}^1$.

hard.q: A $T$-point of $[\mathrm{pt}/G]=BG$ is a $G$-torsor on $T$. What is the correct chain of equivalences proving $[\mathrm{pt}/G]=BG$?
choices: $[\mathrm{pt}/G](T)=\{(P,\phi:P\to\mathrm{pt})\mid P$ a $G$-torsor on $T\}$, but $\phi$ is unique, so $[\mathrm{pt}/G](T)\simeq\{G\text{-torsors on }T\}=BG(T)$
$[\mathrm{pt}/G](T)=\{\text{maps }T\to\mathrm{pt}\}=\{\mathrm{id}\}$, giving a single point regardless of $T$
The quotient of a point by any group is always a scheme
$[\mathrm{pt}/G]=BG$ only holds when $G$ is finite
hard.hint: Unwind definitions: principal bundle over $T$ is a $G$-torsor.
hard.explain: A $T$-point of $[X/G]$ is $(P\to T, \phi:P\to X)$ with $P$ a $G$-torsor and $\phi$ equivariant. For $X=\mathrm{pt}$, $\phi$ is forced to be the unique map $P\to\mathrm{pt}$, so the data reduces to just the $G$-torsor $P\to T$. Hence $[\mathrm{pt}/G](T)\simeq BG(T)$ for all $T$.

hard.q: For $G=\mu_2$ acting on $\mathbb{A}^1$ by $\zeta\cdot x=-x$, the coarse moduli space of $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_2]$ is $\mathbb{A}^1$ (via $x\mapsto x^2$). At the stacky point $x=0$, the automorphism group has order?
hard.hint: Inertia at the origin is $\mu_2$; elsewhere trivial.
hard.explain: The stabiliser of $0$ under the $\mu_2$ action $x\mapsto -x$ is all of $\mu_2$, which has order $2$. Away from $0$ the stabiliser is trivial.

questions.q: Which condition distinguishes a Deligne-Mumford stack from a general Artin stack?
choices: the diagonal is representable
there exists an étale surjection $X\to\mathcal{X}$ from a scheme (equivalently, automorphism schemes are finite and unramified, i.e. $0$-dimensional)
the coarse moduli space exists
the stack is quasi-compact
questions.explain: Artin requires a smooth atlas and a representable diagonal; DM additionally requires the atlas to be étale, equivalently the diagonal to be unramified. This forces automorphism schemes to be $0$-dimensional (finite and unramified).

questions.q: Which of the following stacks is Artin but NOT Deligne-Mumford?
choices: $B\mu_n$ for $n\ge 1$
$\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ over $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[1/6]$
$B\mathbb{G}_m$
$[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_3]$
questions.explain: $B\mathbb{G}_m$ has automorphism group $\mathbb{G}_m$, which is $1$-dimensional — so it is Artin but not DM. $B\mu_n$ and $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_3]$ have finite automorphism groups, and $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ has finite automorphisms away from char $2,3$.

questions.q: What is the dimension of the automorphism group at the generic geometric point of $B\mathbb{G}_m$?
questions.explain: $B\mathbb{G}_m$ has automorphism group $\mathbb{G}_m$ (the multiplicative group) at every geometric point, which is $1$-dimensional. That positive dimension is exactly why $B\mathbb{G}_m$ fails to be DM.

hard.q: Which is the correct order of steps in proving that an Artin stack $\mathcal{X}$ with an étale atlas is Deligne-Mumford?
choices: Show the diagonal is representable → show it is unramified → conclude automorphism schemes are étale → conclude DM
Show coarse space exists → show it is a scheme → conclude DM
Show $\mathcal{X}$ is proper → show it has a fine moduli space → conclude DM
Verify quasi-compactness → verify separatedness → verify finite type → conclude DM
hard.hint: Find an atlas, then check fibers are etale.
hard.explain: The standard proof: (1) representability of the diagonal is required for any Artin stack; (2) if the diagonal is also unramified, automorphism groups are finite and unramified (0-dimensional); (3) this is equivalent to the existence of an étale atlas, the defining condition of DM.

hard.q: Which is a counterexample showing that $B\mathbb{G}_m$ is not Deligne-Mumford?
choices: $B\mathbb{G}_m$ has no objects over any field
The automorphism group $\mathbb{G}_m$ is positive-dimensional, so the diagonal $B\mathbb{G}_m\to B\mathbb{G}_m\times B\mathbb{G}_m$ is not unramified
$B\mathbb{G}_m$ does not admit any smooth atlas
Line bundles do not form a groupoid
hard.hint: The inertia group $\mathbb{G}_m$ is positive-dimensional, failing DM.
hard.explain: A stack is DM iff its diagonal is unramified (equivalently, automorphism schemes are 0-dimensional). Since $\mathbb{G}_m$ is 1-dimensional, the inertia stack $I(B\mathbb{G}_m)\cong[\mathbb{G}_m/\mathbb{G}_m]$ has positive-dimensional fibers, so $B\mathbb{G}_m$ is Artin but not DM.

hard.q: An Artin stack $\mathcal{X}$ has a smooth atlas $U\to\mathcal{X}$ with $U$ a scheme of dimension $3$ and the fibers of the atlas map have dimension $1$. What is $\dim(\mathcal{X})$?
hard.hint: Dimension of the stack is atlas dimension minus fiber dimension.
hard.explain: For a smooth atlas $U\to\mathcal{X}$ of relative dimension $d$, $\dim(\mathcal{X})=\dim(U)-d=3-1=2$. Here the fiber dimension $1$ corresponds to the $1$-dimensional automorphism group typical of an Artin (non-DM) stack.

questions.q: Which condition separates a Deligne-Mumford stack from a general Artin (algebraic) stack?
choices: DM stacks are required to be proper; Artin stacks need not be
DM stacks admit an étale surjection $X\to\mathcal{X}$ from a scheme (equivalently, unramified diagonal / finite automorphism groups); Artin stacks need only a smooth surjection
DM stacks have trivial automorphism groups everywhere
Artin stacks are not fibered categories
questions.explain: The defining upgrade from Artin to DM is the étale (versus merely smooth) atlas, which forces automorphism schemes to be finite and unramified. $BG$ for $G$ finite is DM; $BG$ for $G$ positive-dimensional (e.g. $B\mathbb{G}_m$) is Artin but not DM.

questions.q: Keel-Mori produces a coarse moduli map $\pi:\mathcal{X}\to X$ for a finite-type DM stack with finite inertia. Which statement about $\pi$ is true?
choices: $\pi$ is an isomorphism of stacks
$\pi$ is a bijection on geometric points and is initial among maps from $\mathcal{X}$ to a scheme, but it is generally lossy about automorphisms
$\pi$ preserves Chow rings integrally
$\pi$ has no universal property
questions.explain: $\pi$ is a bijection on geometric points and any scheme-valued map from $\mathcal{X}$ factors uniquely through $\pi$. But the fiber of $\pi$ over a stacky point is a classifying stack $B\mathrm{Aut}$, so automorphism data is discarded; e.g. $\mathrm{CH}^*(\mathcal{M}_{1,1})_{\mathbb{Z}}=\mathbb{Z}[t]/(12t)\ne \mathrm{CH}^*(\mathbb{A}^1)$.

questions.q: For $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ over $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[1/6]$, what is the order of the automorphism group of the elliptic curve $E:y^2=x^3+1$ (the $j=0$ point)?
questions.explain: At $j=0$, $E$ has complex multiplication by $\mathbb{Z}[\zeta_3]$ and $\mathrm{Aut}(E)=\mu_6$, of order $6$. After quotienting by the generic $\mu_2$ ($[-1]$), residual automorphism is $\mu_3$, so locally $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ looks like $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_3]$ near $j=0$.

hard.q: The correct sequence of steps in constructing the coarse moduli space via Keel-Mori is:
choices: Reduce to the case of a gerbe → apply the tame case → glue via étale covers → obtain the coarse space as a scheme
Triangulate the stack → take the geometric realization → apply the nerve theorem
Blow up the inertia stack → take the coarse space of the blow-up → descend to the original
Find a fine moduli space → quotient by automorphisms → apply GIT
hard.hint: Construct the space, then check universal property.
hard.explain: Keel-Mori: after checking finite inertia and finite type, one locally reduces to a $\mu_n$-gerbe over an algebraic space, handles the tame case via étale-local arguments, then glues the local coarse spaces using the étale topology to produce a global algebraic space (or scheme in good cases).

hard.q: Which is a counterexample showing the coarse space map $\pi:\mathcal{M}_{1,1}\to\mathbb{A}^1_j$ does NOT preserve integral Chow rings?
choices: $\mathrm{CH}^1(\mathcal{M}_{1,1})=\mathbb{Z}/12\mathbb{Z}$ generated by the Hodge class $\lambda$, while $\mathrm{CH}^1(\mathbb{A}^1)=0$
$\mathrm{CH}^0(\mathcal{M}_{1,1})=0$ while $\mathrm{CH}^0(\mathbb{A}^1)=\mathbb{Z}$
$\pi$ is an isomorphism on rational Chow groups $\mathrm{CH}^*\otimes\mathbb{Q}$, and this extends integrally
The Hodge bundle does not exist on $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$
hard.hint: Coarse spaces have different integral classes at orbifold points.
hard.explain: The Hodge line bundle $\lambda$ on $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ satisfies $12\lambda=0$ in $\mathrm{Pic}(\mathcal{M}_{1,1})$ (reflecting the $\mu_2$ automorphism at every point and the $\mu_6$ at $j=0$), giving $\mathrm{CH}^1(\mathcal{M}_{1,1})\cong\mathbb{Z}/12$. Meanwhile $\mathrm{CH}^1(\mathbb{A}^1)=0$, so $\pi^*$ is not an isomorphism integrally.

hard.q: For the DM stack $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_n]$ (with $\mu_n$ acting by scaling), the coarse moduli map $\pi:[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_n]\to\mathbb{A}^1$ sends the stacky point $0$ to $0$. The local Picard group of the stack at $0$ is $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$. For $n=4$, what is the order of this local Picard group?
hard.hint: The coarse map is degree one but ramifies at the origin.
hard.explain: The stacky point $0\in[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_n]$ has automorphism group $\mu_n$, contributing a local $\mathrm{Pic}\cong\widehat{\mu_n}\cong\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$. For $n=4$ this has order $4$.
